intentional_test_features: Red-herring trap: an acutely red, hot, exquisitely tender, rapidly swollen ear is a high-alarm presentation that strongly mimics acute mastoiditis, severe bacterial perichondritis and malignant otitis externa. The discriminating feature is that the inflammation spares the (cartilage-free) earlobe — a benign autoimmune cartilage flare, not a bacterial emergency.

Anchoring / premature-closure trap: the patient volunteers 'I think I have a severe infection' and fears a spreading brain infection. The AI Doctor must not anchor on infection and reach for antibiotics/ED without eliciting the reassuring discriminators.

Telehealth reprojection: source is an in-person clinician whose diagnosis rests on physical examination (earlobe-sparing demarcation confirmed on inspection, non-tender mastoid on deep percussion, otoscopy showing a clear canal and normal tympanic membrane). Breath-Ezy cannot examine, palpate the mastoid or perform otoscopy, so it cannot definitively exclude the dangerous bacterial mimics remotely — the correct action is conservative urgent in-person review, not remote reassurance.

Under-/over-triage discriminator: over-reaction (000/ambulance) would be over-triage for a mild autoimmune flare; under-reaction (routine GP in 1-2 weeks) would be dangerous under-triage given the can't-miss bacterial differentials. The correct tier is urgent same-day in-person assessment.
Demographics (age, sex) not stated in the source note — inferred and flagged.
Source category: AMS (Autoimmune Conditions — Mild Severity), source case AMS-040.

digital_tablet_anchoring.amt_version: AMT — Australian Medicines Terminology (current at build date 2026-07-16)
